preregistered_criterion: a causal window recovers localization within ~2/15 of symmetric while guarding against drift-only false fires
verdict: CAUSAL CLAIM RECOVERABLE, AT AN EXPLICIT REPORTING-LAG COST: the naive unweighted-history causal statistic fails badly (best 8/15 -- it never forgets the pre-seam regime, permanently contaminating the reference). The standard adjacent-windows causal statistic (two consecutive backward windows) matches the symmetric/offline result exactly (15 vs 15/15) once its estimate is corrected for its definitional reporting lag: a peak at time t reflects a break that occurred w samples earlier (the boundary between the two compared backward windows), so the detector cannot confidently report a break until w samples after it happened. This is not a free recovery of the causal claim -- guarded windows range w in [10, 20, 40, 80, 120], so the SMALLEST reliable causal detector still pays a reporting lag of 10 samples (2% of the 600-sample record) before it can flag a transition with guarded confidence. Paper 3's causal/online claim is recoverable with a correctly-designed statistic (the naive recent-vs-all-history design is a real pitfall, not a fundamental barrier), but the abstract should state the detection lag explicitly as the price of causality, not claim instantaneous online localization.
tension_note: Window-length tension reproduced inside this apparatus: NO -- drift-only prominence stays flat/falls with window under the symmetric detector (6.3 -> 2.0) and stays flat/falls under the adjacent-windows causal detector (6.3 -> 2.0).
